section.title: The Day of Vengeance
section.sectionRationale: This short, startling oracle answers the question Isaiah 61 left open. A figure approaches from Edom, garments stained crimson like a winepress treader, and identifies Himself as mighty to save. He has trodden the nations alone in judgment because no one else would help. It is the day of vengeance that chapter 61 named but did not yet enact, now pictured in full, sobering color.
section.thesis: The same LORD who saves also judges alone, treading down the nations in righteous wrath when He finds no one to help Him, and this solitary, blood-stained victory is the same divine warrior John later sees returning as Christ, robed and treading the winepress of God's fury.
section.frameNote: Two movements: who is this coming from Edom, crimson-stained, mighty to save; and I trod the winepress alone, for the day of vengeance was in My heart.

units.label: Who is this coming from Edom?

verses.text: Who is this coming from Edom, from Bozrah with crimson-stained garments? Who is this robed in splendor, marching in the greatness of His strength? “It is I, proclaiming vindication, mighty to save.”
Why are Your clothes red, and Your garments like one who treads the {{gat|winepress}}?

units.label: I have trodden the winepress alone
units.note: the day of vengeance, the year of redemption

verses.text: “I have trodden the winepress alone, and no one from the nations was with Me. I trampled them in My anger and trod them down in My fury; their blood spattered My garments, and all My clothes were stained.
For the day of vengeance was in My heart, and the year of My redemption had come.
I looked, but there was no one to help; I was appalled that no one assisted. So My own arm brought Me salvation, and My own wrath upheld Me.
I trampled the nations in My anger; in My wrath I made them drunk and poured out their blood on the ground.”

gat.gloss: Winepress, the vat where grapes were trodden underfoot to release their juice, the stained garments of the treader a natural, vivid picture. Here the image is turned to judgment, treading down the nations as grapes are trodden, an image John draws on directly for Christ's return in Revelation 19:15, He treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty.

basic.context: A lone figure approaches from Edom, His garments stained crimson like a winepress treader. Asked why, He answers that He has trodden down the nations alone in judgment, since no one else would help, and that this was the day of vengeance He had determined and the year of His people's redemption.
basic.hermeneutics: This is deliberately jarring after the gentle mercy of chapters 60 to 62; it insists that the same God who saves also judges, and that His judgment, like His salvation, required Him to act utterly alone. The chapter must be read alongside chapter 61's unfinished sentence, the day of vengeance He named but paused before enacting there is fully pictured here.
basic.originalLanguages: The image is drawn from the gat, winepress, where grapes are trodden until their juice, like blood, stains the treader's garments. The same verb for treading down nations recurs in Revelation, where Christ treads the great winepress of the wrath of God.
basic.typology: This solitary, blood-stained warrior returning from judgment on the nations is the same figure John sees in Revelation 19, a rider called Faithful and True, whose robe is dipped in blood, treading the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty.
basic.christ: Revelation 19:11-16 pictures the returning Christ in language drawn directly from this passage, a warrior on a white horse, robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress of God's wrath, called Faithful and True, King of kings and Lord of lords. Isaiah 63 is the Old Testament's clearest picture of Christ's second coming as righteous Judge, distinct from His first coming as the suffering Servant of chapter 53.
basic.god: God's justice is not a passive withholding, it is an active, righteous response to persistent evil, and He does not delegate it, He acts alone, unaided, precisely because no human agent is righteous enough to execute it rightly. His wrath and His redemption are named together, the day of vengeance and the year of redemption are one and the same act.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 19:11-16 pictures Christ's return using the exact imagery of this passage, robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress. Revelation 14:19-20 describes the great winepress of God's wrath. Isaiah 34:5-8 similarly pictures judgment on Edom as a day of vengeance.
basic.oneStory: The same Servant who was crushed silently in chapter 53 returns here as a warrior who crushes His enemies, and the Bible's whole story holds both truths without contradiction: Christ came once to bear judgment for sinners who trust Him, and He will come again to execute judgment on those who never turned from rebellion.

context.literary: A tightly compressed dramatic dialogue, a watchman or the prophet asking who approaches, the Warrior answering in the first person, structured almost like a scene from a play, standing in sharp contrast to the gentle, celebratory tone of the immediately preceding chapters.
context.historical: Edom, Israel's persistent enemy and kinsman-nation descended from Esau, repeatedly judged elsewhere in the prophets for cruelty toward Israel, Obadiah, Amos 1:11-12, here stands as a representative example of the nations under God's righteous judgment.
context.canonical: Becomes the direct source for John's vision of Christ's second coming in Revelation 19, one of the clearest Old Testament to New Testament links describing the final judgment aspect of Christ's return, distinct from His first advent as suffering Servant.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that the same LORD who saves His people will also personally and decisively judge the nations that opposed Him, acting alone because His own righteousness required it and no human agent could rightly share in it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is vivid prophetic description of a future act of divine judgment; it prescribes no human action, though its sobering tone implicitly calls readers to take God's justice seriously rather than presuming only on His mercy.

debates.question: Is the warrior in Isaiah 63:1-6 the same figure as the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53?

views.label: The same person, Christ, seen in a different role and moment
views.case: Revelation 19 explicitly identifies the rider treading the winepress as Christ returning in judgment, showing that the one who suffered silently in His first coming is the same one who judges decisively at His second coming, two acts of the same person at different times.

views.label: A distinct portrayal of God as divine warrior, not necessarily the same 'moment' as the Servant
views.case: Some read this simply as YHWH Himself acting as divine warrior against Edom, a recurring Old Testament motif, Isaiah 34, without needing to identify Him specifically as the same figure as the Servant of chapter 53.

debates.bearing: Christian reading, informed directly by Revelation 19's explicit borrowing of this imagery for Christ's return, holds both roles together in the one person of Christ: the Servant who bore judgment silently at His first coming, and the Judge who executes judgment openly at His second, without contradiction, since perfect justice and perfect mercy both belong to Him.

study.originalLanguages: The image is drawn from the gat, winepress, where the treader's garments are naturally stained by the crushed grapes, here turned to a picture of the nations crushed in judgment; the same treading imagery reappears explicitly in Revelation's vision of Christ's return.

typology.type: The lone Warrior, robe stained crimson, treading the winepress, verses 1-3
typology.fulfillment: Christ's second coming, robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress of God's wrath, Revelation 19:11-16.

typology.type: The day of vengeance and the year of redemption, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment and consummated salvation accomplished at Christ's return.

study.christ: Revelation 19:11-16 pictures the returning Christ in terms drawn directly from this passage, a rider on a white horse called Faithful and True, His robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty, and crowned King of kings and Lord of lords. Isaiah 63 is the Old Testament's clearest anticipation of Christ's second coming as righteous Judge, standing deliberately alongside chapter 53's picture of Christ as suffering Servant, one person, two comings, mercy first offered, justice finally executed.
study.god: God's wrath is not an embarrassment to be explained away, it is the necessary counterpart to His love for the oppressed and His hatred of persistent, unrepentant evil; He acts alone in judgment because His justice is too perfect to be delegated, and because, as with salvation, no one else was found able to help.

crossReferences.note: Christ's second coming pictured with the exact imagery of this passage, robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress.
The great winepress of the wrath of God, echoing this passage's judgment imagery.
A similar oracle of judgment on Edom as a day of the LORD's vengeance and recompense.
Edom's judgment tied to the coming day of the LORD against all the nations.

study.oneStory: The same LORD who saves His people also judges the nations that oppose Him, and He does both alone, unaided, because His righteousness allows no substitute. Revelation 19 shows this is no different figure than Christ Himself, who bore judgment silently as the Suffering Servant at His first coming and will execute judgment openly, robe stained crimson, when He returns.

quiz.q: Why are the garments of the figure approaching from Edom stained crimson?

options.text: He has trodden down the nations alone in righteous judgment, like treading grapes in a winepress.
options.note: Verses 2-3 explain the winepress image as judgment on the nations.

options.text: He has been wounded and defeated in battle.
options.note: The text describes Him as victorious, mighty to save, not defeated.

options.text: He is wearing ceremonial red robes with no connection to judgment.
options.note: The color is real, but the text explicitly ties it to trodden blood in judgment, not mere ceremonial dress.

options.text: He has been harvesting grapes for a feast, unrelated to judgment.
options.note: The winepress image is explicitly reinterpreted as trampling the nations in wrath.

quiz.q: Why did the Warrior act alone, according to verse 5?

options.text: He looked for someone to help and found no one, so His own arm brought Him salvation.
options.note: Verse 5 explicitly states no one was found to help or assist.

options.text: He refused all offers of help out of pride.
options.note: The text says no one was available to help, not that help was refused.

options.text: The nations had already surrendered before He arrived.
options.note: This is the opposite of the scene; the passage describes active trampling and judgment, not prior surrender.

options.text: Angels fought alongside Him the entire time.
options.note: The text explicitly says no one from the nations, or anyone else named, assisted Him.

quiz.q: How does Revelation 19 use the imagery of Isaiah 63:1-6?

options.text: It applies the same imagery, a robe dipped in blood, treading the winepress, to Christ's second coming as righteous Judge.
options.note: Revelation 19:11-16 draws directly on this passage's imagery for the returning Christ.

options.text: It rejects any connection between Christ and images of judgment.
options.note: Revelation 19 explicitly connects Christ's return to this judgment imagery.

options.text: It applies this imagery only to a human political ruler, not Christ.
options.note: The rider imagery could suggest a human king, but Revelation 19 names Him Faithful and True, King of kings and Lord of lords, identifying Him as Christ.

options.text: It uses entirely unrelated imagery about a wedding feast only.
options.note: While Revelation 19 includes wedding imagery elsewhere, the specific winepress and blood-stained robe imagery echoes Isaiah 63 directly.

quiz.q: How should this passage be understood alongside Isaiah 53's Suffering Servant?

options.text: As two roles of the same Christ, who bore judgment silently at His first coming and will execute judgment openly at His return.
options.note: Revelation 19's identification of Christ as the returning Judge holds both roles together consistently.

options.text: As proof that the Suffering Servant and this Warrior can never be the same person.
options.note: Christian reading, following Revelation 19, holds these as two comings of the same Christ.

options.text: As contradictory pictures that cancel each other out.
options.note: The tension is real and worth wrestling with, but Scripture presents both mercy and justice as consistent, complementary aspects of God's character in Christ.

options.text: As entirely unrelated passages with no bearing on each other.
options.note: Both describe the same divine Servant and Judge at different stages of His mission.

questions: This passage insists that God's justice is real and personal, not distant or abstract. Does your view of God include this side of Him, or only His mercy?
The Warrior looked for someone to help and found no one. Where in your life or your world do you feel the weight of standing nearly alone for what is right?

The day of vengeance and the year of redemption are called the same moment in verse 4. How does it change your view of justice to know that, for those in Christ, judgment and redemption meet at the same cross rather than opposing each other?

Revelation 19 shows the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 is the same Christ who returns as Judge in Isaiah 63. How does living with real expectation of Christ's return, not just as Savior but as Judge, shape the urgency of your life today?

Edom stands here as an example of persistent, unrepentant hostility toward God's people. Is there a persistent, unrepented pattern of opposition to God in your own life you have not yet reckoned with seriously?

translationNotes.note: Bozrah was Edom's capital city; the wordplay between Bozrah and the Hebrew word for grape harvest, batsir, strengthens the winepress imagery that follows.
Year of My redemption pairs judgment and redemption as a single act; the same year that judges the nations is the year Zion's people are finally and fully redeemed.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 63:1-6; no em dashes present in this passage.
